## scout
Scout MCP — semantic code search. The repo is indexed; tools are pre-loaded. Do NOT call `ToolSearch` or shell out to grep/rg/ag/ack/ugrep/fd/find/ls -R — Scout replaces them.

**First call routes by query shape — pick before you call:**
- Architecture / lifecycle / "how does X work" / change plan / "trace Y" / enumerate-or-map → `memory.search` (cache check), then `investigate` if incomplete (`intent: "change"` for change plans, `"understand"` for orientation).
- "Where is X defined" → `go_to_definition`.
- "Who calls X" / "trace usages of X" → `find_references` or `call_graph` (up to 5 levels).
- "What's in file F" / list members of class/enum → `file_outline`.
- "Tell me about symbol X" (def + callers + callees) → `explain_symbol`.
- "What's the blast radius of changing X" → `impact`.
- "Top-level structure" / "list domains" → `architecture_overview`.
- "Top N files / symbols / flows" → `top_files` / `top_symbols` / `list_flows` (CodeRank-ranked; pair AFTER `investigate` for orientation context).
- Concept / NL / unfamiliar identifier → `search`.
- Multiple known identifiers / files / symbols → batch with `queries:` / `files:` / `symbols:` (up to 10).
- Rename across files → `rename(symbol, new_name, dry_run: true)`, review tiers, re-call with `dry_run: false`. Atomic; refuses on collision.
- Migrate Rust field type (`String → Arc<str>` etc.) → `field_audit` (save fingerprint) → `field_migrate(... apply: false)` (preview) → same call with `apply: true`.
- Code/PR review → `memory.search` + `changes.detect(scope: "compare", base_ref: "main")` + `investigate(intent: "change")` + batched `investigate.expand` on risky changed symbols + `impact` for blast radius + `changes.affected_tests` (verify CI doesn't silently skip env-gated tests) + narrow `dead_code` near diff. Treat `Auto-resolved` / `Also defined` markers as review-blocking until disambiguated with `file:`.
- Migration / refactor / multi-vendor sweep ("every X", "vendored copies", "version drift across A/B/C") → after `investigate`, run ONE path-distinct … [truncated]

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

Base directory for this skill: /Users/mukuls/dev/firefly-platform/.claude/worktrees/eval-case-1-inline-magic-20260528T231832Z-unamended/.claude/skills/squirrel-code-review # Squirrel / FFP Code Review Conducts a comprehensive code review of an FFP PR or branch using a generic review checklist plus auto-detected path-scoped rules from `.claude/rules/`. Companion to the lighter-weight `code-review` skill — that one covers post-edit self-review and `Agent:` PR-comment conventions; this one covers full PR/branch review with a report. ## Constraints - **NEVER switch branches.** Use `git show :path/to/file`, `git diff main...`, or the GitHub API. Preserve the user's working state. - Only switch branches if the user **explicitly requests** it. - When posting review comments or replies on GitHub PRs, prefix with `Agent:` (or the agent's name if known, e.g. `Claude:`, `Codex:`) so it's clear the response came from an AI, not the user (GitHub API posts under the user's account). ## Input Parsing | Argument | Mode | How to Get Files | | ---------- | -------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | (none) | Current branch | 1. `gh pr view HEAD --json baseRefName` to check for PR
2. If PR exists → `git diff --name-only ...HEAD`
3. If no PR → `git diff --name-only $(git merge-base HEAD main)...HEAD` and note "No PR found — diffing against main. If this is a stacked branch, provide the base branch or PR number." | | `` | Named branch | Same as above: check for PR on that branch first, use its base. Fall back to main only if no PR exists. | | `` | PR review | GitHub API: `gh pr view --json files,baseRefName` | | `` | PR review | Extract PR number from URL, then GitHub API | ## Guardrail Detection Auto-detect which path-scoped rules apply by matching changed files against `paths:` frontmatter: 1. Glob all rule files: `.claude/rules/**/*.md` (recursive — FFP organizes rules by area, e.g. `.claude/rules/squirrel/`, `.claude/rules/analytics/`). 2. Read each file's YAML `paths:` frontmatter. Rules without `paths:` are unconditional (already in context — no extra read needed). 3. For each changed file in the diff, check which rule's path patterns match (globs are repo-root-relative; brace expansion supported). 4. Load all matching rules into the review context. If no path-scoped rules match (e.g. changes only in `tools/` or `docs/`), proceed with generic review only — unconditional rules in `.claude/rules/` are still in effect via auto-injection. ## Rule Slippage Detection (Critical severity) A loaded rule isn't a reading exercise — it's an enforceable contract. **Every constraint in every loaded rule is a hard gate**. After loading the matching rules in step 3 above, treat each rule's body as a checklist of MUST/MUST NOT statements and verify the diff against every one of them. **Any violation is reported as Critical severity in the report's "Critical Issues (Must Fix)" section** — not as a suggestion, not as maintainability, not as a nit. How to enforce a rule against a file: 1. **Extract the constraints.** Read the rule's body and pull out every imperative — "MUST", "MUST NOT", "Never", "Always", "only", "do not", "banned", explicit allowed/forbidden tables. Bullets that look like advice still count if they are framed as rules. If a constraint is ambiguous, prefer the stricter reading. 2. **Check the diff against each constraint.** For each changed line in a file the rule applies to, ask: does this introduce or perpetuate a violation of any extracted constraint? Pre-existing violations on lines the diff _does not touch_ are out of scope (call them out under "Maintainability Suggestions" at most). Pre-existing violations on lines the diff _does_ touch are in scope — the author had a chance to fix them and didn't. 3. **Cross-file invariants.** Some rules describe boundary contracts (e.g. UI must consume `MainService` only; types must not depend on higher layers; presentation files have no logic). Verify the import graph and call sites of changed files, not just the line-level diff. Use Scout (`mcp__scout__find_references`, `mcp__scout__call_graph`, `mcp__scout__impact`) when the rule is about reachability or layer boundaries. 4. **Test/spec rules count.** Rules under `.claude/rules/test.md`, `.claude/rules/e2e-test.md`, banned-antipattern lists for E2E, etc. apply to test files. Mocked-module patterns, `waitForTimeout`, shadow walks, networkidle, `describe.skip` all become Critical findings when the rule banning them is loaded. Reporting format for each violation: > **Critical — Rule slippage: `` constraint ""** > > File: `:` What the rule says: What the diff does: > Required fix: A PR with even one rule slippage cannot earn an **Approve** verdict — the minimum is **Request Changes**. Authors can disagree with a rule, but the reviewer's job is to flag the slippage; rule-edit conversations happen in a separate PR against `.claude/rules/`, not by quietly shipping code that violates a loaded rule. ## Review Workflow ### Step 1: Determine source Parse the argument per the Input Parsing table. Get the list of changed files and the full diff. ### Step 2: Gather context Display: branch/PR name, file count, ticket (extract from branch name or PR title — FFP uses `[FFP-NNNNN]` prefixes), commit count. ### Step 3: Load review criteria - Read `references/generic-review-checklist.md` — always loaded - Run guardrail detection (see above) — load matching path-scoped rules ### Step 4: Read changed files - Current branch: read from working directory - Other branches: `git show :path/to/file` - PRs: GitHub API or `git show` Focus on changed sections. For large files, focus on changed line ranges. ### Step 5: Conduct review **To add a new check, add it to the relevant `.claude/rules/` file** — the guardrail detection step loads it automatically. Do not add checks directly to this skill. Apply the generic checklist and any loaded path-scoped rules to all changed files. For each file, apply only the rules whose `paths:` match that file. Favor Scout (`mcp__scout__*`) over shell-based search when looking for parallel implementations, callers of changed symbols, or magic-value duplicates. **Run rule slippage detection on every loaded rule** (see "Rule Slippage Detection" below). This is not optional — every imperative in every matching rule is checked against the diff, and every violation is a Critical finding. ### Step 6: Generate report Structure output per `references/report-template.md`. ### Step 7: Complete Present the report with numbered findings grouped by severity. For PR reviews, offer to post comments to GitHub (with `Agent:` prefix per the constraints above). ## Quality Gates Check against project-defined targets in `CLAUDE.md` or path-scoped rules. For FFP: - TypeScript builds via `tsc -b` (project references). Type-check the affected sub-project, e.g. `tsc -b src/studio/tsconfig.json`. - `pnpm lint:ts` (oxfmt + eslint), `pnpm lint:md`, `pnpm lint` (full). - Test commands per the affected package's `package.json`. ## Scope Flag PRs combining unrelated changes. Suggest splitting into separate PRs (bug fix, feature, refactoring). ## DRY checks ### Within-method duplication Look for repeated code blocks within the same function that differ only by parameter values. If a pattern appears 2+ times, flag it for extraction to a helper. This includes repeated validation sequences, similar conditional patterns, and copy-pasted blocks with renamed variables. Report as **Maintainability**. ### Cross-file duplication When new files are added, check whether similar code already exists elsewhere in the package. Common signals: a new file that mirrors an existing one in structure; utility functions (file ops, validators, formatters) re-implemented instead of imported from a shared module. **Only flag duplication within the same package** — cross-package similarity may be intentional to avoid coupling. Use Scout `semantic_code_search` or `keyword_search` to find parallel implementations. ## Magic values Hardcoded literals that should be named constants are a maintainability issue: - Check whether a constant already exists in the codebase for any hardcoded numeric or string literal (Scout `keyword_search` works well). - Check imports in the file — if a utility package is imported, look for constants it exports. - Flag as maintainability if the same raw value appears in 2+ places or if a named constant clearly exists. ## Verdict criteria | Verdict | When | | --------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | Approve | No critical issues, no rule slippage, functional concerns minor or discussed | | Request Changes | Any rule slippage from a loaded `.claude/rules/` file, OR critical correctness/security issues, OR missing required elements | | Comment Only | Suggestions or questions without blocking merge | ## Error handling - Branch doesn't exist → display error, suggest checking branch name - PR not found → display error with PR number/URL used - No changes found → inform user branch is up to date with main - File read fails → note in review, continue with other files ## Important notes - For large PRs (>20 files), group review by component/directory. - Focus on substantive issues over style nitpicks. - Acknowledge good practices alongside issues. - When new code is added, ALWAYS search for similar existing code — never review in isolation.

# Source Root

See `.claude/skills/architecture/SKILL.md` for overall repo architecture.

# src/ Root Folders

The following top-level folders exist under `src/`. **Do not add new root folders** — if new code does not fit an existing folder, discuss
with the team first.

| Folder          | Purpose                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| --------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `applications/` | Self-contained feature applications (image generation, video generation, music generation, etc.), each with their own `elements/` and `services/`                                                        |
| `elements/`     | Shared Lit web components and presentation files used across multiple applications. Also contains global `styles/` (design tokens, theme, reset) and shared `assets/` (model thumbnails, banners, logos) |
| `functions/`    | Shared utility functions, pure helpers, and developer tools (deeplink generator, image-editing utilities). No side effects, no services                                                                  |
| `home/`         | The Firefly home website — a full standalone site, kept here rather than `applications/` due to its scope                                                                                                |
| `openapi/`      | Generated API clients for Colligo and other backend services. Do not hand-edit — regenerate via build scripts                                                                                            |
| `scripts/`      | Build-time and codegen scripts (not shipped runtime code). Run via `pnpm` tasks                                                                                                                          |
| `services/`     | Shared platform services (auth, entitlement, analytics, host, etc.) consumed by multiple applications                                                                                                    |
| `studio/`       | The Firefly Studio website — a full standalone site, kept here rather than `applications/` due to its scope                                                                                              |
| `types/`        | Shared TypeScript type definitions used across the platform                                                                                                                                              |

# Squirrel

## Load before editing (skills — read if your task matches)

- `ecs` — `Database.Plugin` authoring (property order, components-as-schemas, resources-as-defaults)
- `observe` — `Observe.withMap` / `.withFilter` / `.fromProperties` / `.toPromise`
- `lit` — binding elements, `useObservableValues`, verbNoun action callbacks
- `presentation` — `*-presentation.ts` exports `render` + optional `unlocalized`, nothing else
- `architecture` — layered binding → presentation → service interface → service impl
- `test` — mock-free; real `Database.create(plugin)`

## Squirrel FATALs (code-review blockers)

### No bare `.flickValue` or inline flick math outside `types/flick-time/**`

```ts
// BANNED
t.flickValue + 100;
Math.max(min, Math.min(max, t)); // clamp re-implementation
pixelsPerFlick * t.flickValue; // inline px math

// ALLOWED
TimeUtils.add(t, TimeUtils.fromSeconds(1));
TimeUtils.clamp(t, min, max);
PixelUtils.timeToPx(t, effectivePixelsPerFlick);
```

Branded types: `TimelineTime`, `TimelineDuration`, `TrackItemTime`, `TrackItemDuration`, `MediaTime` — refuse mixing at the type level.

### MainService interface is hand-written; every plugin capability must be exposed

See `.claude/rules/squirrel/main-service-interface.md` for the full contract — the `ReturnType<typeof create>` ban (TS7056 + internal
leakage), the per-capability interface entry requirement for new transactions and computeds, and the pre-merge diff check.

### Alignment, playhead writes, trim writes, and raw speed math live only at named edges

Alignment (`alignToFrame` / `alignToFlicksPerFrame`) outside the four edge sites, writes to `playheadTime` / `explicitTrim*` /
`explicitTimeOffset` outside their ECS-boundary sinks, and raw `* speed` / `/ speed` outside the named `timelineTimeToTrackItemTime` /
`trackItemTimeToTimelineTime` conversions — all four patterns are mechanically forbidden. Four Vitest grep gates under `types/flick-time/`
will fail the suite on a new violation:

- `no-aligns-outside-edges.test.ts`
- `no-playhead-writes-outside-sink.test.ts`
- `no-trim-writes-outside-sink.test.ts`
- `no-raw-speed-math.test.ts`

See `src/applications/squirrel/types/flick-time/ARCHITECTURE.md` for the full invariant contract (the four allowed edges, the align-at-delta
algebra, the split exemption, and Hz parity references).

## Mechanical bans (guaranteed-loaded — empirically fragile under context pressure)

### ECS plugins (`types/main-service/create/plugins/**`)

- Property order in `Database.Plugin.create({…})`:
  `extends, services, components, resources, archetypes, computed, transactions, actions, systems`. Wrong order throws at runtime.
- `components: { foo: Foo.schema }` or `{ foo: { default: X as Type, ephemeral?: true } }` — **never** plain-object shapes like
  `{ x: 0, y: 0 }`.
- `resources: { foo: { default: X as Type } }` — **never** bare `foo: 0`. Use `null as unknown as T` for late-init.
- `transactions: (t, args) => void` — pure, synchronous, **no `await`**, no cross-transaction calls, **at most one transaction per action**.
- `computed: (db) => Observe<T>` — factory returning `Observe`; never raw values.
- `db` parameter type: `CoreStateDatabase = Omit<Database.Plugin.ToDatabase<typeof coreStatePlugin>, 'computed'>`. **Never `db: any`**.
- Every plugin under `create/plugins/` carries `/** @internal */`.
- Foundation/ ↔ computed/ ↔ transactions/ ↔ actions/ must follow the declared DAG (see `plugins/foundation/README.md`). Enforced by
  `plugins/foundation-import-dag.test.ts` — cross-layer imports outside the allowed edges fail the suite.

### UI elements (`elements/**`)

See `.claude/rules/squirrel/binding-element-compliance.md` for the full binding-element contract (shape, `@property` whitelist by class
role, observed-values discipline, no logic in render or callbacks, lifecycle-via-hooks, no timers/emitters/broadcast channels, no direct DB
reads, fire-and-forget actions, presentation contract, `verbNoun` callback names).

### Tests (`*.test.ts`)

- **Never** `vi.mock`, `vi.fn`, `jest.mock`, `jest.fn` or any module-level mock. Use dependency injection + plain-object fakes.
- Real `Database.create(plugin)` for transaction/computed tests. Never mock the ECS.
- Clamp/fit assertions must probe the boundary (both sides). Canonical-input-only clamp tests are vacuous.

## Data-flow layers

Every state change flows:
`UI event → action → transaction → archetype mutation → computed observer → public view-model (main-service.ts) → useObservableValues re-render`.
If your diff writes state but skips a layer, justify in the PR description.

## Canonical analogs (grep first)

- **Transaction**: `types/main-service/create/plugins/transactions/create-track.ts`
- **Element**: `elements/track/track-element.ts` + `track-presentation.ts` + `track.css.ts`
- **Computed**: `types/main-service/create/plugins/computed/effective-pixels-per-flick.ts`
- **Branded time**: `types/flick-time/flick-time.ts`

## Out of scope

No drive-by fixes to autoscroller, scroll-padding trim hack, or other known-issue areas. Keep diffs localized.

# AI Guidance Standards

Claude natively discovers `.claude/skills/`, `.claude/rules/`, and `CLAUDE.md` from any directory up to the repo root whenever it reads a
file in that subtree. Author guidance close to the code it describes — no build steps or manual wiring required.

→ [Claude Code memory docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory)

## Three-tier system

| Mechanism     | File location                    | When active                                                                          | Use for                                                                     |
| ------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **CLAUDE.md** | `<dir>/CLAUDE.md`                | Always, for any file in subtree                                                      | Hard constraints, short architectural orientation, pointers to rules/skills |
| **Rules**     | `.claude/rules/<name>.md`        | Auto-injected when open file matches `paths:` glob; **unconditional if no `paths:`** | Enforcing constraints on specific file types or always-on invariants        |
| **Skills**    | `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | On-demand — user invokes or Claude self-invokes by name                              | Reusable patterns, deep reference, repeatable workflows                     |

**Rules are the right choice when** the guidance should apply automatically based on what file the user is editing — no explicit invocation
needed. Rules without a `paths:` field load unconditionally at every session start (same behavior as CLAUDE.md but in a dedicated file).

**Skills are the right choice when** the guidance is invoked on-demand for a specific task. A skill may reference a rule or another skill by
path.

**CLAUDE.md is the right choice when** something must always be present for any work in a subtree, regardless of which file is open.

---

## Structure

```
<repo root>/
  CLAUDE.md                    ← always-present context for this subtree
  .claude/
    rules/
      <name>.md                ← injected on file-path match, or unconditional if no paths:
    skills/
      <name>/
        SKILL.md               ← reusable knowledge or workflow, invoked on demand
```

`CLAUDE.md` and `.claude/skills/` are discovered hierarchically — Claude walks up from the current file's directory to the repo root,
picking up each level. Place them at the most specific directory where they apply.

`.claude/rules/` is root-only. Rules must live at the repo root `.claude/rules/` because `paths:` globs are evaluated relative to the repo
root and there is a single discovery location. Subdirectory organization within `.claude/rules/` (e.g. `.claude/rules/squirrel/`) is fine.

---

## CLAUDE.md

Loaded automatically whenever Claude reads any file in that directory or below. Use sparingly — every line is always in context. Target 200
lines max; longer files reduce adherence.

**Belongs here:**

- Hard constraints or invariants that must always be active for any work in this subtree
- Short architectural orientation specific to this subtree
- Pointers to key rules or skills

**Does not belong here:**

- Constraints that only apply to specific file types → put in a rule with `paths:`
- Reusable patterns or workflows → put in a skill
- Verbose documentation → keep it tight
- Guidance that only applies to one file or interface → inline comment in source

**Root `CLAUDE.md` is the exception** — it holds MCP config and tool bootstrap only.

---

## Rules

Rules are markdown files in `.claude/rules/`. They load automatically — no invocation required.

### Frontmatter

```yaml
---
paths:
  - 'src/**/*-presentation.ts'
  - 'src/**/*.css.ts'
---
```

- **With `paths:`** — injected only when the open file matches a glob (relative to repo root). Multiple patterns are OR'd. Brace expansion
  works: `src/**/*.{ts,tsx}`.
- **Without `paths:`** — loads unconditionally at every session start, like a global CLAUDE.md entry. Use for universal invariants (e.g.
  type safety, core behavior).

Rules contain their guidance inline. Do not use `@include` — it is not a supported feature.

### When to use rules

- Enforcing a constraint on all files of a certain type (e.g. all presentation files, all test files)
- Enforcing conventions scoped to a path (e.g. all files under `src/applications/squirrel/`)
- Universal invariants that should always be in context → rule without `paths:`
- Anything you want applied automatically without the user or Claude having to remember to invoke it

---

## Skills

Reusable knowledge and workflows invokable by name. On every session start, Claude loads the `name` and `description` of all discovered
skills — full content only enters context when invoked.

**Skills survive context compaction**: the most recent 5,000 tokens of each invoked skill are carried forward automatically (25,000 token
combined budget). Re-invoke a skill after a long session to restore its full content.

### Frontmatter fields

```yaml
---
name: my-skill # matches folder name exactly
description: What this skill does # load-bearing: Claude reads this to decide when to self-invoke (1,536 char limit)
when_to_use: additional trigger phrases appended to description
disable-model-invocation: true # only the user can invoke (not Claude) — use for side-effect workflows like deploy or eval
user-invocable: false # only Claude can invoke (not the user) — use for background knowledge
---
```

All fields except `name` are optional.

### Naming

- Folder name is the canonical skill name: `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`
- `name` frontmatter must match the folder name exactly
- Use kebab-case, be specific: `binding-element` not `ui`

### Description

The description is load-bearing — it is the primary signal Claude uses to decide whether to self-invoke. Write full sentences describing
when the skill applies and what it provides. Use `when_to_use` for additional trigger phrases that complement the description. Vague
descriptions mean the skill is never invoked.

### Content

- **Tool-agnostic** — no Claude-specific or Cursor-specific syntax
- Skills may reference related rules or skills by relative path
- Skills are not duplicates of rules — they are on-demand complements

---

## Placement quick reference

| Guidance type                                    | Where it goes                                |
| ------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| Always-needed context for a subtree              | `CLAUDE.md` in that directory                |
| Constraint enforced by file type/location        | `.claude/rules/<name>.md` with `paths:` glob |
| Universal invariant (always in context)          | `.claude/rules/<name>.md` without `paths:`   |
| Reusable pattern or workflow, on-demand          | `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`             |
| User-only workflow (deploy, eval, side effects)  | Skill with `disable-model-invocation: true`  |
| Constraint specific to one interface or function | Inline comment in source                     |
| MCP config / tool bootstrap                      | Root `CLAUDE.md` only                        |

---

## Governance

Enforced via CODEOWNERS — two tiers:

**AI Governors** (`@Adobe-Firefly/Firefly-Platform-AI-Governors`) review:

- `**/CLAUDE.md` — all CLAUDE.md files at any depth
- `.claude/` — root canonical skills and rules (wide blast radius)
- `src/.claude/` — sits above all team directories (higher risk)

**Team owners** review (via existing CODEOWNERS):

- Nested `.claude/` under their own directories — scoped, limited blast radius

**Review checklist:**

1. `CLAUDE.md` is concise (≤200 lines) and contains only always-present context — no file-type constraints, no reusable patterns
2. Rules with `paths:` are scoped to the right file types; rules without `paths:` are genuinely universal
3. Rules contain their guidance inline — no `@include`
4. Skills have specific, unambiguous descriptions (full sentences); `when_to_use` added where helpful
5. Side-effect or eval skills use `disable-model-invocation: true`
6. No content duplicated across skills or rules
7. Skill content is tool-agnostic
8. Guidance lives at the most specific directory that makes sense

# Type namespace pattern

Single import surface per type: `<type-name>/<type-name>.ts`. Schema in `-schema.ts`, namespace re-export from `public.js`. Types live in
the `types/` layer.

## Authority

This rule defines the canonical pattern. **Follow this standard for all new and refactored type folders.** The codebase may contain legacy
patterns (e.g., inline `export namespace` in shared files) that do not conform — do not copy those; follow this rule instead.

## Naming conventions

- Constants are **camelCase** (`flicksPerSecond`, not `FLICKS_PER_SECOND`) — strong team preference
- Each function/type gets its own file matching its name

## Constant types

| Visibility | Definition                      |
| ---------- | ------------------------------- |
| private    | Single-file only, not exported  |
| exported   | Sole export from eponymous file |
| public     | Re-exported from public.ts      |
| internal   | Exported, not in public.ts      |

## Constraints

- `<type-name>/<type-name>.ts` is the only public import surface
- Export a single type alias: `export type <type-name> = <type>` (or `export type * from "./<type-name>-types.js"` when the type lives in a
  separate file)
- Schema-based types: schema in `<type-name>-schema.ts`, named `schema` (not `<type-name>Schema`), derive with
  `Schema.ToType<typeof schema>`
- Export namespace: `export * as <type-name> from "./public.js"`
- In public.ts, re-export every public constant file
- Filenames map deterministically to single export:
  - `foo.ts` → `export type Foo = ...`
  - `do-bar.ts` → `export function doBar() { }`
  - DO NOT append other suffixes like `-type` etc.
- Function files: name by purpose only (e.g., `add.ts`, `sub.ts`). **Do not prefix** with the type name — the folder provides the namespace
  context.

## Anti-patterns (do not copy)

- **`export namespace <TypeName> { ... }`** — Use `export * as <TypeName> from "./public.js"` instead.
- **Prefixing function files with the type name** (e.g., `vec2-add.ts`) — Use `add.ts`; the folder provides context.
- **Importing from `public.js` or `-schema.js`** outside the type folder — Consumers must import only from `<type-name>/<type-name>.ts`.

## Consumers

**Never:**

- Import from `public.js` outside the type namespace folder
- Import from `<type-name>-schema.js` outside the type namespace folder

**Do:**

- `import { Volume } from "../../types/volume/volume.js"`
- For schema: `import { PlayerMark } from "../../types/player-mark/player-mark.js"`, then `PlayerMark.schema`

## Schema pattern example

```ts
// src/types/player-mark/player-mark-schema.ts
export const schema = { enum: ['X', 'O'] } as const;

// src/types/player-mark/public.ts
export { schema } from './player-mark-schema.js';

// src/types/player-mark/player-mark.ts
import { Schema } from '@adobe/data/schema';
import { schema } from './player-mark-schema.js';
export type PlayerMark = Schema.ToType<typeof schema>;
export * as PlayerMark from './public.js';
```

## Example: `Point` type refactor

Starting file `src/types/point.ts`:

```ts
export type Point = { x: number; y: number };
export const length = ({ x, y }: Point) => Math.hypot(x, y);
export const add = ({ x: x1, y: y1 }: Point, { x: x2, y: y2 }: Point): Point => ({ x: x1 + x2, y: y1 + y2 });
```

After applying the namespace pattern:

```ts
// src/types/point/point.ts
export type Point = { x: number; y: number };
export * as Point from './public.js';

// src/types/point/public.ts
```

# Type namespace pattern

Single import surface per type: `<type-name>/<type-name>.ts`. Schema in `-schema.ts`, namespace re-export from `public.js`. Types live in
the `types/` layer.

## Authority

This rule defines the canonical pattern. **Follow this standard for all new and refactored type folders.** The codebase may contain legacy
patterns (e.g., inline `export namespace` in shared files) that do not conform — do not copy those; follow this rule instead.

## Naming conventions

- Constants are **camelCase** (`flicksPerSecond`, not `FLICKS_PER_SECOND`) — strong team preference
- Each function/type gets its own file matching its name

## Constant types

| Visibility | Definition                      |
| ---------- | ------------------------------- |
| private    | Single-file only, not exported  |
| exported   | Sole export from eponymous file |
| public     | Re-exported from public.ts      |
| internal   | Exported, not in public.ts      |

## Constraints

- `<type-name>/<type-name>.ts` is the only public import surface
- Export a single type alias: `export type <type-name> = <type>` (or `export type * from "./<type-name>-types.js"` when the type lives in a
  separate file)
- Schema-based types: schema in `<type-name>-schema.ts`, named `schema` (not `<type-name>Schema`), derive with
  `Schema.ToType<typeof schema>`
- Export namespace: `export * as <type-name> from "./public.js"`
- In public.ts, re-export every public constant file
- Filenames map deterministically to single export:
  - `foo.ts` → `export type Foo = ...`
  - `do-bar.ts` → `export function doBar() { }`
  - DO NOT append other suffixes like `-type` etc.
- Function files: name by purpose only (e.g., `add.ts`, `sub.ts`). **Do not prefix** with the type name — the folder provides the namespace
  context.

## Anti-patterns (do not copy)

- **`export namespace <TypeName> { ... }`** — Use `export * as <TypeName> from "./public.js"` instead.
- **Prefixing function files with the type name** (e.g., `vec2-add.ts`) — Use `add.ts`; the folder provides context.
- **Importing from `public.js` or `-schema.js`** outside the type folder — Consumers must import only from `<type-name>/<type-name>.ts`.

## Consumers

**Never:**

- Import from `public.js` outside the type namespace folder
- Import from `<type-name>-schema.js` outside the type namespace folder

**Do:**

- `import { Volume } from "../../types/volume/volume.js"`
- For schema: `import { PlayerMark } from "../../types/player-mark/player-mark.js"`, then `PlayerMark.schema`

## Schema pattern example

```ts
// src/types/player-mark/player-mark-schema.ts
export const schema = { enum: ['X', 'O'] } as const;

// src/types/player-mark/public.ts
export { schema } from './player-mark-schema.js';

// src/types/player-mark/player-mark.ts
import { Schema } from '@adobe/data/schema';
import { schema } from './player-mark-schema.js';
export type PlayerMark = Schema.ToType<typeof schema>;
export * as PlayerMark from './public.js';
```

## Example: `Point` type refactor

Starting file `src/types/point.ts`:

```ts
export type Point = { x: number; y: number };
export const length = ({ x, y }: Point) => Math.hypot(x, y);
export const add = ({ x: x1, y: y1 }: Point, { x: x2, y: y2 }: Point): Point => ({ x: x1 + x2, y: y1 + y2 });
```

After applying the namespace pattern:

```ts
// src/types/point/point.ts
export type Point = { x: number; y: number };
export * as Point from './public.js';

// src/types/point/public.ts
export * from './length.js';
export * from './add.js';

// src/types/point/length.ts
export const length = ({ x, y }: Point) => Math.hypot(x, y);

// src/types/point/add.ts
export const add = ({ x: x1, y: y1 }: Point, { x: x2, y: y2 }: Point): Point => ({ x: x1 + x2, y: y1 + y2 });
```

## Execute

1. **Follow this rule** — Do not copy patterns from existing code; apply this standard.
2. Identify constants in the current file.
3. Extract them into the standard pattern.
4. Identify all consumers of public namespaces.
5. Check each consumer: ensure imports use `<type-name>.ts` only, never public.js or -schema.js.
6. Ensure unit tests exist per function file, splitting as needed.

# Squirrel scaling-cost rubric

Apply these pattern checks before generating or accepting new Squirrel code. Each row is a symptom that has already produced measurable pain
in this tree.

## Symptom table

| Symptom                                                                                                                    | Why it hurts                                                                                                                    | Do instead                                                                                           |
| -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Binding element with >2 private cache fields or any `@state` beyond UI-local                                               | Hook-closure leak: caches exist because a hook's callback closed over stale props                                               | Give the hook a ref-read API (live read via `useRef`) or push logic into a controller                |
| `extends SquirrelElement` class body > 200 LOC                                                                             | Concerns not extracted — drag, pointer, selection, cursor should each be their own module                                       | Extract to a controller or dedicated action; keep the binding thin                                   |
| "Pure function factory" closure with >10 mutable vars or nested mutating fns                                               | It is a class in disguise — you lose method dispatch, gain closure-capture bugs                                                 | Name it a class, or model it as a `Database.Plugin` sub-plugin                                       |
| `shadowRoot?.querySelector('.foo')` on a sibling/child component                                                           | Couples caller to another component's internal class names; renames break silently                                              | Export a DOM-contract helper on the owning element (e.g. `TrackEl.getItemHosts()`)                   |
| New plugin file under `plugins/transactions/` or `plugins/computed/` shipped without a matching entry in `main-service.ts` | Capability is unreachable from UI OR interface drifts and drift test goes red                                                   | Run `pnpm gen:main-service` (codegen); commit the diff                                               |
| Per-frame drag loop body > 150 LOC in an action file                                                                       | Copy-paste across drag variants — 4× maintenance cost per change                                                                | Use the shared `drag/frame-state-machine/` primitives                                                |
| `"Hz parity:"` comment citing a Hz `file:line` without a snapshot reference                                                | Hz is a moving target; cites rot the moment Hz refactors                                                                        | Cite `docs/squirrel/hz-behavior/snapshot.md §<section>` instead                                      |
| Hidden DOM element (`hidden` attr, display:none) with a `TODO(...)` comment                                                | Dormant markup that will break silently during CSS refactors                                                                    | Gate render path behind `squirrelFeatures.<flag>` or delete                                          |
| Test file > 800 LOC                                                                                                        | Un-debuggable at 2 am; navigation burns review budget                                                                           | Split by behavior-under-test into per-subject spec files                                             |
| `as unknown as Entity` (or any `as unknown as <brand>`) in a test                                                          | Missing fixture factory — every site drifts independently                                                                       | Use `createTestEntity()` / `createTestTrackId()` from `main-service/test-utils/`                     |
| `lb as any`, `window as any`, or any `as any` in production                                                                | Bypasses the narrow-type firewalls already present in `litebrite-bridge.ts` et al.                                              | Route through the typed factory; if the type is missing, add it there                                |
| Magic pixel/ms constant (`50`, `16`, `170`) inline with no named export                                                    | Centralization forces one-place update during a design tweak                                                                    | Export from `elements/timeline/timeline-constants.ts` (or sibling)                                   |
| New presentation file that exports anything besides `render` (and `unlocalized`)                                           | Violates the presentation contract — breaks `Parameters<typeof render>[0]` consumers                                            | Move helpers into a sibling non-presentation file; keep presentation exports minimal                 |
| Subscribe-then-immediately-unsubscribe sync read inside `render()`                                                         | Fakes a sync read, breaks reactivity — banned by `ui-data-access.md`                                                            | Add to `useObservableValues` or resolve via an action                                                |
| `vi.mock` / `vi.fn` / module-level mock in any test                                                                        | Banned by CLAUDE.md; masks ECS contract drift                                                                                   | Real `Database.create(plugin)` + plain-object fakes at boundaries                                    |
| Export-loop using `TimeUtils.timelineTime(seconds)` instead of `TimeUtils.fromSeconds.timelineTime(seconds)`               | `timelineTime(v)` takes raw flicks; passing seconds encodes every frame at position ≈ 0                                         | Use `TimeUtils.fromSeconds.timelineTime(s)` for any second-based input                               |
| Async encode path with no timeout on the first `source.add()` call                                                         | `VideoEncoder.isConfigSupported()` is lazy-called on frame 0 and can hang forever on some GPU configs, freezing the modal at 0% | Wrap frame-0's `add()` in `Promise.race([add, rejectAfter(30_000)])`                                 |
| Progress modal with no `data-*` numeric attribute (e.g. `data-progress`)                                                   | E2E can only observe that the modal mounted — silent encode bugs (wrong time unit, stuck encoder) go undetected                 | Bind `data-progress=${Math.round(progress)}`; add an `expect.poll` assertion that it advances past 0 |

## When a row fires during code generation

1. Stop before writing the file and call out which row triggered.
2. Quote the table row verbatim — the user can then accept, reject, or redirect.
3. If accepting the violation, leave a one-line comment explaining why (scope-limited, feature flag not yet in place, etc.). A comment with
   no reason is a red flag at review time.

## When a row fires during review

Block the PR. The rule exists because each pattern has already cost the team time.

## Not a rubric for refactor scope

This rule governs _new_ code. Existing violations are being migrated in waves through dedicated PRs — do not drive-by-fix unrelated
violations inside an unrelated PR. Scope discipline (`.claude/rules/squirrel/...` / `feedback_scope_discipline` memory) takes precedence.

content.content:
# Flick-time contract (Squirrel)

Every time value has a `coordinate` brand (`timeline`, `media`, `trackItem`) and a `semantic` brand (`time` instant, `duration` span). Never
use raw `number` for a time quantity. Never touch `.flickValue` except inside `types/flick-time/**` or `ecs-boundary/**`.

## Speed crosses only between TimelineTime and TrackItemTime

And only via the two named anchored conversions:

```ts
TimeUtils.timelineTimeToTrackItemTime(tl, tlAnchor, tiAnchor, speed): TrackItemTime
TimeUtils.trackItemTimeToTimelineTime(ti, tiAnchor, tlAnchor, speed): TimelineTime
```

No inline `* speed` or `/ speed`. No `* effectiveSpeed` or `/ effectiveSpeed`. Enforced by `types/flick-time/no-raw-speed-math.test.ts`.

## Alignment happens only at four edges

- **Pixel → time (entry):** `PixelUtils.pxToAlignedTimelineTime` (round). Only for consumers that can't go through the playhead sink (snap
  thresholds, drag math).
- **Media ingestion (entry):** `writeSourceDuration(t, id, v, frameRate)` — floor-aligns. Throws on `frameRate <= 0`.
- **Playhead writes (exit):** `writePlayheadTime(t, time)` — round-aligns. Throws on `frameRate <= 0`. Callers pass raw; sink aligns.
- **Trim-helper delta (internal):** `trim-freeform-item.ts`, `trim-to-playhead.ts` — align the `TrackItemDuration` delta **before** clamp;
  never align the result. `timeOffset` absorbs sub-frame drift at `speed ≠ 1`.

Every other `alignToFrame` / `alignToFlicksPerFrame` call is forbidden. Enforced by `types/flick-time/no-aligns-outside-edges.test.ts`.

## Trim sinks (`writeExplicitTrimIn`, `writeExplicitTrimOut`, `writeExplicitTimeOffset`) do not align

Callers deliver pre-aligned values; sinks just write. Writes go through `createBrandedStore(t).update(id, {...})` or the named sink
functions directly. No `t.update(id, { explicitTrimIn: ... })` outside the ECS-boundary. Enforced by
`types/flick-time/no-trim-writes-outside-sink.test.ts`.

## Playhead is write-through-sink only

No `t.resources.playheadTime = ...` / `db.resources.playheadTime = ...` outside `ecs-boundary/resource-writes.ts` and the `branded-store`
proxy. Enforced by `types/flick-time/no-playhead-writes-outside-sink.test.ts`.

## Split is an exemption

`splitAtPlayhead` (keyboard `Cmd+B`) and `splitAtTime` (click-to-split tool) cut at the user's `TimelineTime` without aligning.
`trimOut_left` / `trimIn_right` may be off the TrackItem grid at `speed ≠ 1`. The transaction does NOT re-align them. The split tool does
NOT move the playhead — it routes through `splitAtTime(time)`, passing the click's timeline position directly.

## Forward-compat: time ramps

`TrackItemTime` and `MediaTime` are identity in flicks today but will diverge when time ramps land. Use `TimeUtils.trackItemTimeFromMedia` /
`mediaTimeFromTrackItem` (and the `Duration` siblings) at every boundary — identity today, ramp-aware tomorrow. Never cast
`as TrackItemTime` / `as MediaTime`.

## Full contract

See `src/applications/squirrel/types/flick-time/ARCHITECTURE.md` for the complete invariant contract, the align-at-delta algebra, sink
inventory, known anti-patterns, and Hz parity references.

content.content:
# Main Service parity — plugin side

Companion to `squirrel/main-service-interface.md`. That rule covers the interface file (`main-service.ts`); this one covers the plugin files
every capability ultimately lives in. Both load together when the drift crosses the boundary — which is the only way drift lands.

**Background.** PR #9125 (scaling-debt PR 4) added a codegen + drift test that compared `main-service.ts` against a generated
`plugin-shape-manifest.json`. It was reverted (#9134) because PR #9107 (Skimmer) shipped with a drift the interface rule did not catch:
`toggleSkimmer` was declared under `actions:` in `main-service.ts` but lived in `plugins/transactions/`, and three skimmer resources were
declared in the `SquirrelResources` schema but defined only inline in `core-state-plugin.ts`. No reviewer flagged it. The drift test was the
only mechanical check. When that test failed mass coverage runs, the fastest unblock was to revert the test, not fix the Skimmer labeling.
The codegen can be re-landed; this rule is the human-reviewable backstop that prevents the same shape of drift next time.

## The three invariants

### 1. Section matches source directory

A capability named `X` under `main-service.ts::transactions.X` must live in `plugins/transactions/X.ts` or be declared inline in a plugin's
`transactions: { ... }` block. Same for `actions.X → plugins/actions/X.ts`, `computed.X → plugins/computed/X.ts`.

Wrong-section declarations compile (TypeScript only checks the signature, not the source location) but fail at the capability boundary: an
action has async context and returns `Promise<void>`; a transaction is synchronous and returns `void`. Mislabeling a transaction as an
action invites UI code to `await` a synchronous call — silent no-op in the happy path, surprise in rejection paths. The mismatch also
bypasses the per-gesture undo frame the action layer wraps around transactions.

Canonical example from the Skimmer revert: `toggleSkimmer` was declared as an action in `main-service.ts` but the source at
`plugins/transactions/toggle-skimmer.ts` was a transaction. The drift test flagged it; reviewers didn't.

### 2. Resource schema is the single source of truth

`SquirrelResources` in `main-service.ts` is the declaration. The inline `resources: { ... }` block in `core-state-plugin.ts` (and any
sub-plugin that declares resources) must list the same keys with matching types. Neither side is allowed to carry a key the other does not.

Mismatches are silent:

- Key in schema, missing from plugin → `db.observe.resources.<key>` returns undefined forever; UI sees stale defaults.
- Key in plugin, missing from schema → the resource works at runtime but is invisible on the public surface; UI code that touches it can
  only do so via `any`-widened reads (a type-safety rule violation) or by routing through a computed.

Canonical example: `skimmerEnabled` / `skimmerTime` / `skimmerVisible` were added to `SquirrelResources` in the Skimmer PR but defined only
as inline resource declarations in `core-state-plugin.ts:119`. The schema-based drift check reported them as missing; they were present in
the plugin the whole time, just not in the shape the check knew how to read.

### 3. No phantom capabilities

Every entry under `transactions:` / `actions:` / `computed:` / `services:` in `main-service.ts` must have a backing plugin file (or inline
plugin declaration) in the same PR. Don't stub the interface ahead of the plugin, don't carry dead declarations.

A "declare now, wire later" entry is a runtime throw waiting to happen: `service.actions.toggleSkimmer(...)` compiles, but at call time the
action-plugin lookup fails and the UI gets a cryptic "no such action" error instead of the feature.

Canonical example: Skimmer's `main-service.ts` declared `handleSkimmerDisappear`, `processSkimmerHover`, and `toggleSkimmer` as actions with
no matching files under `plugins/actions/`. The feature still worked because the three transactions existed and the e2e specs
`skimmer-toggle.spec.ts` / `skimmer-hover.spec.ts` bypassed the action layer, but every caller that trusted the interface surface was one
rename away from a runtime null.

## Pre-review audit

When a PR adds or renames anything under `plugins/transactions/`, `plugins/actions/`, `plugins/computed/`, or changes the inline
`resources: { ... }` block of any plugin, the diff must also touch `main-service.ts` in the same commit range, and the three checks above
must hold. A diff that touches only one side of the boundary is either incomplete or is introducing drift.

## Mechanical backstop

The codegen at `scripts/gen-main-service-interface.ts` + the drift test (`main-service-drift.test.ts`) is the automated form of this rule.
The codegen catches drift at CI time; the rule catches it at review time. Review-time is cheaper than waiting on a coverage run to surface a
red drift assertion.

**Codegen allowlists must be kept in sync with plugin additions.** The generator uses two hand-maintained allowlists because a full
TypeScript program would make the drift test too slow:

- `ACTION_SOURCES` — every `plugins/actions/*.ts` record file and every `plugins/**/*-plugin.ts` inline-plugin file that declares
  `actions: { ... }`. Add the new entry in the same PR that adds the action file.
- `CORE_STATE_RESOURCES` — every key in `core-state-plugin.ts::resources`. Add the new entry in the same PR that adds the resource.

# Main Service parity — plugin side

Companion to `squirrel/main-service-interface.md`. That rule covers the interface file (`main-service.ts`); this one covers the plugin files
every capability ultimately lives in. Both load together when the drift crosses the boundary — which is the only way drift lands.

**Background.** PR #9125 (scaling-debt PR 4) added a codegen + drift test that compared `main-service.ts` against a generated
`plugin-shape-manifest.json`. It was reverted (#9134) because PR #9107 (Skimmer) shipped with a drift the interface rule did not catch:
`toggleSkimmer` was declared under `actions:` in `main-service.ts` but lived in `plugins/transactions/`, and three skimmer resources were
declared in the `SquirrelResources` schema but defined only inline in `core-state-plugin.ts`. No reviewer flagged it. The drift test was the
only mechanical check. When that test failed mass coverage runs, the fastest unblock was to revert the test, not fix the Skimmer labeling.
The codegen can be re-landed; this rule is the human-reviewable backstop that prevents the same shape of drift next time.

## The three invariants

### 1. Section matches source directory

A capability named `X` under `main-service.ts::transactions.X` must live in `plugins/transactions/X.ts` or be declared inline in a plugin's
`transactions: { ... }` block. Same for `actions.X → plugins/actions/X.ts`, `computed.X → plugins/computed/X.ts`.

Wrong-section declarations compile (TypeScript only checks the signature, not the source location) but fail at the capability boundary: an
action has async context and returns `Promise<void>`; a transaction is synchronous and returns `void`. Mislabeling a transaction as an
action invites UI code to `await` a synchronous call — silent no-op in the happy path, surprise in rejection paths. The mismatch also
bypasses the per-gesture undo frame the action layer wraps around transactions.

Canonical example from the Skimmer revert: `toggleSkimmer` was declared as an action in `main-service.ts` but the source at
`plugins/transactions/toggle-skimmer.ts` was a transaction. The drift test flagged it; reviewers didn't.

### 2. Resource schema is the single source of truth

`SquirrelResources` in `main-service.ts` is the declaration. The inline `resources: { ... }` block in `core-state-plugin.ts` (and any
sub-plugin that declares resources) must list the same keys with matching types. Neither side is allowed to carry a key the other does not.

Mismatches are silent:

- Key in schema, missing from plugin → `db.observe.resources.<key>` returns undefined forever; UI sees stale defaults.
- Key in plugin, missing from schema → the resource works at runtime but is invisible on the public surface; UI code that touches it can
  only do so via `any`-widened reads (a type-safety rule violation) or by routing through a computed.

Canonical example: `skimmerEnabled` / `skimmerTime` / `skimmerVisible` were added to `SquirrelResources` in the Skimmer PR but defined only
as inline resource declarations in `core-state-plugin.ts:119`. The schema-based drift check reported them as missing; they were present in
the plugin the whole time, just not in the shape the check knew how to read.

### 3. No phantom capabilities

Every entry under `transactions:` / `actions:` / `computed:` / `services:` in `main-service.ts` must have a backing plugin file (or inline
plugin declaration) in the same PR. Don't stub the interface ahead of the plugin, don't carry dead declarations.

A "declare now, wire later" entry is a runtime throw waiting to happen: `service.actions.toggleSkimmer(...)` compiles, but at call time the
action-plugin lookup fails and the UI gets a cryptic "no such action" error instead of the feature.

Canonical example: Skimmer's `main-service.ts` declared `handleSkimmerDisappear`, `processSkimmerHover`, and `toggleSkimmer` as actions with
no matching files under `plugins/actions/`. The feature still worked because the three transactions existed and the e2e specs
`skimmer-toggle.spec.ts` / `skimmer-hover.spec.ts` bypassed the action layer, but every caller that trusted the interface surface was one
rename away from a runtime null.

## Pre-review audit

When a PR adds or renames anything under `plugins/transactions/`, `plugins/actions/`, `plugins/computed/`, or changes the inline
`resources: { ... }` block of any plugin, the diff must also touch `main-service.ts` in the same commit range, and the three checks above
must hold. A diff that touches only one side of the boundary is either incomplete or is introducing drift.

## Mechanical backstop

The codegen at `scripts/gen-main-service-interface.ts` + the drift test (`main-service-drift.test.ts`) is the automated form of this rule.
The codegen catches drift at CI time; the rule catches it at review time. Review-time is cheaper than waiting on a coverage run to surface a
red drift assertion.

**Codegen allowlists must be kept in sync with plugin additions.** The generator uses two hand-maintained allowlists because a full
TypeScript program would make the drift test too slow:

- `ACTION_SOURCES` — every `plugins/actions/*.ts` record file and every `plugins/**/*-plugin.ts` inline-plugin file that declares
  `actions: { ... }`. Add the new entry in the same PR that adds the action file.
- `CORE_STATE_RESOURCES` — every key in `core-state-plugin.ts::resources`. Add the new entry in the same PR that adds the resource.

A PR that adds a file under `plugins/actions/` or a key to `core-state-plugin.ts::resources` without updating the matching allowlist is the
exact failure class that killed PR #9125 in #9134 — the Skimmer PR added `plugins/actions/skimmer-actions.ts` and three skimmer resources
without updating the allowlists, the drift test went red on every coverage run, and the fastest unblock was to revert the codegen rather
than to update the allowlists. The allowlists are code, not comments; missing an entry is a bug, not a documentation lapse.

VERDICT: BLOCK

## Summary

**Branch:** `eval/scaling-costs/case-1-inline-magic-20260528T231832Z-unamended`
**Worktree base:** `f28d69588a5` (already on `f3836351b03 eval: case-1-inline-magic` on top)

If this is a stacked branch, please confirm the base.

**Changed file**

- `src/applications/squirrel/types/main-service/create/plugins/computed/connection-adornment-eval.ts` (new)

**Inline reviewer / mitigation notes**

- No `eval-meta/` sidecar exists; no inline `// reviewer:` annotation, no JSDoc justification, no PR-body mitigation. The branch suffix `-unamended` is consistent with "no author mitigation present." I therefore evaluate the diff as-shipped, with no downgrade to apply.

**Rules loaded by `paths:` match against the diff (auto-injected, verified against this changed file):**

- `.claude/rules/squirrel/plugins.md`
- `.claude/rules/squirrel/main-service-interface.md`

---

## Critical Issues (Must Fix)

### 1. Rule slippage: `.claude/rules/squirrel/scaling-costs.md` — "Magic pixel/ms constant inline with no named export"

**File:** `src/applications/squirrel/types/main-service/create/plugins/computed/connection-adornment-eval.ts:12`
**What the rule says (verbatim row from the symptom table):**
> "Magic pixel/ms constant (`50`, `16`, `170`) inline with no named export … Centralization forces one-place update during a design tweak … Export from `elements/timeline/timeline-constants.ts` (or sibling)."

**What the diff does:** Hard-codes `const dotSize = 4` inside `buildConnectionDotStyle`. `4` is a pixel literal (it goes straight into `width` / `height` in the returned style) with no named export, and "rendered dot size" is exactly the kind of UI-design constant the rule is meant to centralize. The eval branch name (`case-1-inline-magic`) telegraphs that this is the planted finding; under the rule slippage gate the row fires regardless of intent.

**Required fix:** Lift to a named export in `elements/timeline/timeline-constants.ts` (or the nearest sibling constants module — there is no existing `connection-dot-size` export anywhere in the tree, verified via Grep), and import it. If the rule's table row is still in effect (it is — file matches `paths:`), inline `4` cannot ship.

### 2. Rule slippage: `.claude/rules/squirrel/plugins.md` — "Computed Factories"

**File:** `src/applications/squirrel/types/main-service/create/plugins/computed/connection-adornment-eval.ts:1-19`
**What the rule says:**
> "Each computed property gets its own file under `plugins/computed/`. Type `db` as `CoreStateDatabase` … `(db) => Observe<T>` — factory returning `Observe`; never raw values."

**What the diff does:** The file is placed under `plugins/computed/` but is not a computed. It exports a plain pure function `buildConnectionDotStyle(dotTop, leftPx): DotStyle` — no `db: CoreStateDatabase`, no `Observe<…>`. Sibling files in this directory (`effective-pixels-per-flick.ts`, `clip-header-metadata.ts`, etc.) all conform; this one is an outlier. The Squirrel `CLAUDE.md` "mechanical bans" section restates the same shape (`computed: (db) => Observe<T>`), so the violation is doubly loaded.

**Required fix:** If this is genuinely view-model glue for a connection-adornment dot, move it out of `plugins/computed/` to `elements/.../<owning-element>-presentation-helpers.ts` (or wherever its sole caller lives). If it really is supposed to be reactive view-model, rewrite as `(db: CoreStateDatabase): Observe<DotStyle>` per the canonical shape.

### 3. Rule slippage: `.claude/rules/squirrel/main-service-parity.md` — Pre-review audit ("Section matches source directory" / "No phantom capabilities")

**File:** `src/applications/squirrel/types/main-service/create/plugins/computed/connection-adornment-eval.ts` (new) + missing edit in `src/applications/squirrel/types/main-service/main-service.ts`
**What the rule says:**
> "When a PR adds or renames anything under `plugins/transactions/`, `plugins/actions/`, `plugins/computed/` … the diff must also touch `main-service.ts` in the same commit range … A diff that touches only one side of the boundary is either incomplete or is introducing drift."

And from `main-service-interface.md`:
> "Pre-merge check: every new or renamed file under `plugins/{transactions,computed}/` in the diff has a matching added line in `main-service.ts`. A diff that adds a plugin file without an interface entry is either incomplete (the UI call site will never type-check) or the capability is plugin-private (in which case remove the speculative plugin file)."

**What the diff does:** Adds a file under `plugins/computed/` without (a) adding an entry to `main-service.ts::computed`, (b) adding an entry to `plugins/computed/index.ts`'s aggregator (verified — `index.ts` does not re-export `connection-adornment-eval.js`), or (c) marking it `@pluginPrivate`. There is no `MainService.computed.connectionAdornment*` declaration. Per the rule, this is exactly the "speculative plugin file" pattern and must be either fully wired or deleted.

**Required fix:** Either wire the capability through `index.ts` → `computed-plugin.ts` → `main-service.ts::computed` with a hand-written interface line, or remove the file. Half-wired is forbidden by both rules.

### 4. Rule slippage: Global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` — "Use the current calendar year for copyright headers in new files"

**File:** `src/applications/squirrel/types/main-service/create/plugins/computed/connection-adornment-eval.ts:1`
**What the rule says (global):**
> "Use the current calendar year for copyright headers in new files. Do not use outdated years."

**What the diff does:** The new file has no copyright header. Every sibling under `plugins/computed/` carries `// © 2026 Adobe. All rights reserved. See /COPYRIGHT for details` (verified on `effective-pixels-per-flick.ts`, `clip-header-metadata.ts`, `track-item-render-snapshot.ts`).

**Required fix:** Prepend `// © 2026 Adobe. All rights reserved. See /COPYRIGHT for details` as the first line.

---

## Maintainability Suggestions

### 5. Dead code — exported function has zero call sites

`buildConnectionDotStyle` and `connection-adornment-eval` are referenced nowhere in the repo (`grep -r 'buildConnectionDotStyle\|connection-adornment'` returns only the new file). The file is not aggregated in `plugins/computed/index.ts` either, so it is unreachable from the plugin graph. Either wire a real consumer in this PR or delete the file. Lands as "phantom capability" under `main-service-parity.md`'s third invariant.

### 6. Filename does not match a single deterministic export (`namespace.md`)

`namespace.md` requires: `do-bar.ts → export function doBar()`. The file `connection-adornment-eval.ts` exports `buildConnectionDotStyle` and a local type `DotStyle` — neither corresponds to the filename. Either rename the file to `build-connection-dot-style.ts`, or move the function to a namespace folder (`types/dot-style/build.ts` etc.) per the rule. The `-eval` suffix is also non-standard for production code.

### 7. Local `DotStyle` type duplicates standard CSS-style shape

`{ top, left, width, height: number }` is recurrent across the timeline ruler/playhead/connection-adornment surface. Before adding another local definition, check `elements/timeline/**` for an existing `RectStyle` / `BoxStyle` / `OffsetSize` export and reuse it. This is the within-package DRY check called out in this skill's "Cross-file duplication" section.

### 8. `dotTop` parameter name is ambiguous

`dotTop` is a pixel offset from the connection-adornment's coordinate root, but neither the parameter name nor the return type carries any unit information. Adding `: number /* px */` is a one-character improvement; promoting the parameters to a typed `{ topPx, leftPx }` object also avoids positional-argument bugs at the call site (which doesn't exist yet — see #5 — but will).

---

## Acknowledgements

- `DotStyle` is correctly typed (no `any`, no `as`), and the function is pure / synchronous — the `flick-time.md` rule has nothing to flag here (no `* speed`, no `alignToFrame`, no `playheadTime` write, no `.flickValue`).
- TypeScript's `noUnusedLocals` will not catch issue #5 because `buildConnectionDotStyle` is `export`ed; the right backstop is `gen-main-service-interface` codegen + the `main-service-drift.test.ts` (when re-landed) — exactly the gap `main-service-parity.md` was written to plug, and exactly why review-time enforcement matters.

---

## Verdict rationale

Four rule-slippage Critical findings under loaded `.claude/rules/**/*.md` files (scaling-costs magic-pixel row, plugins computed-factory shape, main-service-parity pre-review audit, copyright-year global rule). Per this skill's verdict matrix, *any* rule slippage forces the minimum verdict to **Request Changes** — that maps to **BLOCK** for the wrapper. No author mitigation note exists in the diff or in `eval-meta/` to weigh against the findings.
